passages.author: Marcus Aurelius
passages.text: Everything harmonizes with me, which is harmonious to thee, O Universe. Nothing for me is too early nor too late, which is in due time for thee. Everything is fruit to me which thy seasons bring, O Nature: from thee are all things, in thee are all things, to thee all things return.
passages.context: An address to Nature, the classic expression of the Stoic acceptance of fate (amor fati).

passages.author: Epictetus
passages.text: Don't demand that things happen as you wish, but wish that they happen as they do happen, and you will go on well.
passages.context: One of the Handbook's shortest maxims, stating non-resistance to events directly.

passages.author: Marcus Aurelius
passages.text: Whatever may happen to thee, it was prepared for thee from all eternity; and the implication of causes was from eternity spinning the thread of thy being, and of that which is incident to it.
passages.context: Marcus frames each event as woven into the order of the whole, a ground for welcoming what comes.
